comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's technically Monday now -- a little past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight tonight Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. 

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Astral nasties that a LBRP won't deal with
comments.content:
I recently spent a week away from home sleeping in a motel. As any good mage does, I performed banishings to clear the space of etheric muck. From the first day I was there, I performed two banishings with the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram a day, morning and night. The first night I was bothered by something appearing like a classic 'grey' alien, which I managed to intimidate enough to get to back off. I fell into a nightmarish lucid dreaming state, remaining apparently in the motel room but being visited by family and other people, culminating in a battle with a particularly menacing floating bean bag. Ragged but determined, the following night when the 'grey' returned, I prayed to a god I have a strong relationship with and was not harassed again that week.

While I am satisfied that prayer has been proven as a successful option, I'd like to know, what other portable ritual or magical tools are there for nuisances that are not dispelled by the LBRP? Does this resilience suggest that I was dealing with a more serious entity than the standard nightmare pest?

- Patrick

replies.title: Re: Astral nasties that a LBRP won't deal with
replies.content: The LBRP is a basic practice suitable for complete beginners, so yes, it doesn't always get rid of everything quickly -- though if you keep performing it in the same place, it'll gradually strip away everything noxious. The LBRP plus the Rose Cross ritual (or the OIW Ritual, which is the Druidical equivalent) will do more. If you've got a location with serious muck, open a temple in full ritual form -- you can use any convenient surface, including the floor, for an altar; a small incense holder fits conveniently in luggage, and any cup will serve for water -- and invoke the protective deity of your choice, then close in form, and that should do it fairly well. Of course natural magic is also always an option -- protective herbs and amulets can make things like that much easier, (My book The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic covers that in detail.)

replies.title: Re: Astral nasties that a LBRP won't deal with
replies.content: Most hotels absolutely forbid incense (and candles), so in that case would essential oils (frankincense, bay laurel, etc) be of use?

replies.title: Re: Astral nasties that a LBRP won't deal with
replies.content: Yes, especially if you dip a stick into them and use it in ritual the way you'd use a stick of lit incense.

comments.title: Organ donation
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,

Here's what I've been pondering last week: what are the magical effects of organ donation (if any)?

Kind regards, Brigyn

replies.title: Re: Organ donation
replies.content: Good question. I don't know that anybody's looked into that.

replies.title: Re: Organ donation
replies.content:
I don’t know about magical effects, but I’ve heard the following anecdote. A middle class white lady of middle age once received a heart transplant. The donor, unbeknownst to her, had been a young black man with an avid interest in basketball. After a lifetime of taking no interest in sports whatsoever, she found herself becoming an enthusiastic television basketball fan.

I heard this from a friend who had seen a program about it, probably on Discovery Channel. I imagine it’s likely to be true. If so, it suggests that organ transplants come with a history that may well influence the recipient.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Organ donation
replies.content: Thank you for the data point, Kevin!

comments.title: Coelbren
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

No questions for the moment; just a comment. I’m back in the UK, and have bought a copy of Coelbren through the local bookshop. I’m reading it with interest (in parallel with Penny Billington’s ‘The Path of Druidry’). Just wanted to mention that when I was growing up, I’d always wondered what the runic symbols were at the base of the memorial stone to Iolo Morganwg (on the wall of the building where he sold radical pamphlets and salvery-free sugar!). Now I know!

Here’s a link to a Wikimedia image of the stone: https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Iolo_Morganwg,_y_Bont-faen.JPG

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content: The Coelbren passage says "Gwir yn erbyn y byd," "The Truth against the World," which was Iolo's motto and (according to him) the traditional motto of the Bards of Glamorgan. Thank you for this!

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content:
"The Truth Against the World" was also the motto that one of my all-time favorite Spiritualists and Occultists, Emma Hardinge Britten (1823-1899), took for her own as early as 1870. Whence she derived it no one knows, but in her early 'teen years she was employed as an entranced clairvoyant by a lodge of highly-placed British occultists. It is likely that the esoteric interests of some members of this lodge extended as far as Iolo's Druidry.

EHB always refers to that magical lodge as the "Orphic" Brotherhood (or Circle) in her writings, but she also states that this was not its actual name. Its members certainly included such esoteric luminaries as Edward Bulwer-Lytton (the novelist), Philip Henry Stanhope, and Richard James Morrison (who is also the eminent astrologer "Zadkiel"). It likely included other occultists who moved in their social circles, for instance, John Varley (the artist), Marguerite Blessington, Richard F. Burton (the explorer), Ernest de Bunsen, and William Alexander Crawford. Our host will know better than I whether any of these men and women also figure in the annals of early 19th-century Druidry.

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content:
Good heavens. Yes, she pretty much had to get it from a Iolo-derived context. I don't happen to know enough about early 19th century Druid circles in Britain to be sure just where the overlap was; none of the names you mention ring a Druidical bell, but there were odd connections running through all kinds of corners of the British occult scene of the time.

"The Truth against the World" was also used by Frank Lloyd Wright, interestingly enough...

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content: Frank Lloyd Wright's mother came from a Welsh Unitarian family (the Lloyd Joneses) that immigrated together with other Welsh Unitarians to plant a small Welsh Unitarian community in Spring Green, Wisconsin.  Returning to Spring Green in 1911, he built himself a famous house that he named Taliesin.  Doubtless some of the Lloyd Jones clan had been reading Iolo while he was growing up there.  --  Robert Mathiesen

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content: That'll explain a thing or two. Iolo was a Unitarian -- I've read, in fact, that half the hymns in the Welsh Unitarian hymnal to this day are by him.

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content:
I didn't know that about Iolo. That explains a lot.

On another note, it appears that Bulwer-Lytton had read Iolo, so there's a possible link through the "Orphic" Brotherhood with Emma Hardinge Britten.

I have seen hints that Bulwer-Lytton had the motto displayed in a mosaic on the floor of the entrance hall to his home, but I haven't reliably sourced that yet. Appanently Tennyson took the same phrase as his motto, too. So "The Truth gainst the World" was in the air during the second half of the 19th century.

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content: Hmm! Fascinating.

replies.title: Re: Coelbren
replies.content: The unsigned comment about Emma Hardinge Britten and her and Iolo Morganwg's shared motto, "The Truth Against the World," was mine. -- Robert Mathiesen

comments.title: Paths of wisdom
comments.content:
I am inching my way toward a magical practice, and since I know some Hebrew, I picked up your book Paths of Wisdom. When I did a tarot reading exploring my interest however, I got the Tower in reverse crossed by the Hermit. Is that at all auspicious?

Also, since the book has been out for some time, what would you change about it if you were to write it again?

Thanks for doing this!

Jay Dee

replies.title: Re: Paths of wisdom
replies.content:
Never ask anyone else to interpret your Tarot readings for you. That's like asking some stranger to get involved in a conversation between you and a lover! I'd encourage you to meditate on the cards, in the context of the rest of the reading, and work out what it means.

As for the book, I'd make a lot of little corrections and revise the basic philosophical stance to reflect my current views, but as a guide to the Golden Dawn Cabala it's stood the test of time quite well. My current take on the Cabala will be covered in a forthcoming sequel to The Celtic Golden Dawn.

comments.title: Just the right amount
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Thanks for your answer about astral projection.

In a magical working how do you make sure you get the right amount of magical energy driving to achieve your goal without going to far. Any goal would be bad if taken to far. I'm cautious about starting to do magic before knowing how to avoid a "sorcerers apprentice" run away kitchen scenario.

How compatible are the mental training methods in W.E. Butler's apprenticed to magic with the druid magic handbook if energy working and wheel of the year are followed from your book.

What is a good source of info on the wheel of the year in more depth than, like is available for the tree of life.

Can a machine like a car possess a kind of spirit? Are there machines and constructs that can and some that can't?

Thank you for hosting this here, its much easier to read than on the blog.

Michael

replies.title: Re: Just the right amount
replies.content:
1) Don't worry about getting too much energy. As a beginner, your constant challenge will be getting enough to have any effect at all.

2) Yes -- Butler's writings have been a major influence on my magical work since day one (his book The Magician: His Training and Work was one of the first two books on magic I ever read) and my practices draw fairly heavily on the tradition in which he worked.

3) There isn't one yet.

4) Some machines do seem to pick up a certain inner dimension, others don't. I don't know of anyone who's worked out why.

replies.title: Re: Just the right amount
replies.content: How would you recommend gaining an understanding of the wheel of the year and the stations on it. Would meditating on the cycle of the seasons and moving out to many other kinds of cycles from there give some understanding?

replies.title: Re: Just the right amount
replies.content: That would be an excellent approach.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection
comments.content: Is there a limit to the number of times the Sphere of Protection can be safely practiced in one day?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Beginners always want to overdo basic ritual practices -- I know I did! Once a day is the right amount -- more is too much, less is too little. Instead of doing it repeatedly, work on learning how to do more complex and interesting ritual work.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a few questions here: you mentioned that western occultism and vegetarianism don't mix. Why is that, and slightly broader: how much does diet influence magic?

I have this uncanny knack for being able to walk into a bookstore and find any book I want, no matter how obscure, old, or otherwise unlikely to be there, as long as I'm there to find something else. This has happened enough times that I'm starting to think there may be something here. Do you have any idea what it might be?

Over at your blog, you said, "A good deal of what’s going on here in the United States would, I think, be diagnosed by a competent Native American medicine person as a mass case of soul loss." What are the symptoms of soul loss?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's not occultism generally, it's ritual magic. Vegetarianism makes you psychically sensitive -- that sounds great, but it can be a serious problem if you live in a modern community (the kind of psychic static that fills most contemporary towns and cities isn't something you want to be sensitized to), and a much worse problem with magic, because magic ups the intensity of the experiences you're going to have. It's as though you were to do something to make your ears more sensitive while taking up rick guitar -- increased intensity plus increased sensitivity can cause actual damage. (Thus one of the best ways to shut your psychism down if you're in a situation where you need to do that is to have a big meal of relatively greasy meat. Barbecue pork ribs are among my go-to meals for this when I'm on the road.)

2) It's called intuition. Everybody has it; you've simply picked up the knack of using it -- which is a good thing. Try doing the same thing in other contexts and see if you can expand the range of ways you can use it!

3) You'll have to consult a medicine person for that, or read some good books on shamanism. That's not a field in which I've specialized.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1: Where does the psychic static come from?

2: The part that makes it hard to use is that I have to want a book and go into a store looking for something else (ex: I found stoic writings while looking for birthday presents for someone). It's a very weird thing, and I'm not sure how to consciously use it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1. The thoughts and feelings of everyone around you.

2. See if you can make it work for something other than books (e.g., you want a certain tool and you go to the hardware store looking for something else).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: So if the psychic static comes from the thoughts and feelings of people around, then that would seem to imply there's always static, but sometimes it's better than other times. Is that accurate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The amount of static is a function of two things -- the total number of people nearby, on the one hand, and the degree to which their minds are full of mental trash, on the other. (A meditating mystic doesn't produce static -- he or she produces the equivalent of a quiet clear tone that makes it easier for those around to think.) For both reasons, things are pretty thick just now.

comments.title: loss of soul
comments.content:
Many thanks for hosting this! my ears pricked up with the discussion of soul loss on ecosophia.

Carl Jung discusses the symptoms somewhat as being clinical depression, but I remember thinking that he skimped a bit on explanations and maintained a quite materialist viewpoint, at least with the material I read. Of course I've also read Carlos Castaneda in my drug soaked teenage years and later read the much more sober accounts of Medicine Grizzly Bear Lake's excellent book _Native Healer_ where he discusses some of his experiences and technique. Still, my understanding is admittedly fuzzy at best and the entire subject strikes me as compelling.

My questions are: 1) do you know of any good resources for researching soul loss and possible ways of addressing this grave problem? I find the sort of anthropological literature where a rationalist is doing, essentially, weird travel literature hard to sift through, so any recommendations are much appreciated! 2) How can you tell if you have lost your own soul or are dealing with someone with with a missing soul or part, what are the signs and symptoms? 3) does magical ritual in the western tradition help retrieve souls -- i.e if I am missing my soul or part can doing the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram help to restore my soul?

replies.title: Re: loss of soul
replies.content: The western magical tradition doesn't include the concept of soul loss. I'd have to sit down with a competent traditional shaman or two and have some long, careful talks before I'd consider myself qualified to say what in western occultism corresponds to the traditional diagnosis of soul loss. As for the literature, what I know of it was mostly cribbed from old ethnographic literature about the coastal Salish people of Washington State, which I read many years ago to try to get a sense of the mythic landscape of the place I was living.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A few weeks ago, you wrote to me in reply to a question I had: "Music (like all the arts) can also be pursued as a deep and intense spiritual practice; if you pay close attention to how your music affects your state of consciousness and that of your listeners, it very quickly approximates magic."

I'm wondering if you wouldn't mind elaborating on that and/or pointing me to a few sources or examples for further reading. I thought about it since then, and I take it to mean partly that art-making partially involves the act of exploring your own behavior/beliefs through the means of another substance (the media you are working with). So if I understand you correctly, you are saying that I should be paying attention to what happens in my consciousness when making or listening to my own music, and watching the feedback that happens as a work of art begins to manifest in the materials. A trivial example: let's say I have a penchant for making melancholy music, then it's worth noticing that and reflecting on why.

Art-making is also a way of exploring the seven laws as you described them in Mystery Teachings - you can see them being put to action in the art that you make.

The other thought that struck me was that artists have often described their art as not being made by them - something takes hold of them and they act as a channel for that art to be made. I don't know if there are parallels with the psychological idea of the flow state - but I've noticed in myself that sometimes my music has just written itself.

I have read descriptions by some artists about how they have been 'taken hold' of something while making art, that they are simply acting as a channel for a work of art to be created. I don't know if there are parallels with the psychological idea of the flow state - but I've noticed in myself that sometimes, rarely, my music has just 'written itself'.

You wrote to someone last week: "The Watcher at the Threshold doesn't take the form of random annoyances. It's an emotional and/or cognitive reaction, taking forms such as boredom, disinclination, being suddenly attracted to a whole flurry of other activities, or the various grades of fear. I'd encourage you to keep trying."

Does this affect people without magical training, too? This sensation happens to me very often when starting a new project, musical or otherwise. My mind has a very familiar set of tricks that it plays on me to avoid actually starting. Thanks in advance!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm not really qualified to say much about music as a spiritual path -- I'm not a musician and so haven't read more than a little on the subject. Joscelyn Godwin's books on the esoteric dimension of music are my go-to resources there. As a writer, I'm very aware of the extent to which my work is a cooperative product of my mind and the imaginal world -- to the extent that my characters will literally sit me down sometimes and explain something that I've missed about them. (Jenny Parrish, one of the two central characters of The Weird of Hali, had to do that when I kept trying to figure out who she was going to end up in a relationship with; she's an asexual, and I didn't get that until she told me.)

As for the Watcher, though, it waits whenever and wherever you go beyond the limits of the little self, and the creative arts are among the ways beyond, so, yes, that's him. Get used to him, learn to give him a nod and a smile as you blow on past him, and everything will work out just fine. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What is the "little self"?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Also known as the lower self or the ego. It's your idea of who you are, as distinct from who you actually are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Get used to him, learn to give him a nod and a smile as you blow on past him, and everything will work out just fine. ;-)"

Oh man, I love this! So much better than getting all dramatic and starting a wrestling match every time you try to pass by.

comments.title: Why pentagrams?
comments.content:
Are pentagrams used in magical workings simply because of the symbolism the mage wants to work with, the elements if I'm not mistaken(Earth, air, fire, water, & spirit)? Or is there something magically unique about the symbol?

Could a ritual with a six or seven pointed star, or a series of triangles, say, with slightly different symbolism work as well?

I recall you saying something about a ritual practiced by many people over time being more effective than one invented and used by one individual(perhaps I'm remembering this inaccurately). Is it that the golden Dawn system was an effective one and the use of it's symbolism gained a sort of 'momentum' after it caught on?

If I understand correctly the sphere of protection makes use of a circle that is spun around the body fast enough to effectively become a sphere, so it seems like it wouldn't be the case of 'this is the only symbol that works'. Though I could understand it being the case that many were tried and a few seem to work better than others...

If it is possible to use a different shape would it matter if the shape used was made up of an unbroken line rather? Like comparing the continuous nature of a pentagram/circle drawing with a 'star of David' for example, which could be two triangles overlaying or interlinking each other depending how you drew it.

I suppose that's a lot of questions...

So, why pentagrams?


Thank you for doing these magic Mondays. Really learn a lot from these threads.

Jason P.

replies.title: Re: Why pentagrams?
replies.content: The pentagram has been a protective symbol since ancient Greek times; the Pythagorean brotherhood used it as one of their symbols. Yes, all the emphasis on the pentagram down through the centuries has given it a lot of power, and its geometry is based on the golden proportion, which has specific magical properties as well. Could you use a different symbol? Yes, of course, but you'd have to put a lot of effort into building up any kind of power in it, and the pentagram already has that due to twenty-six centuries of hard word by occultists.

replies.title: Re: Why pentagrams?
replies.content:
Fascinating, I didn't realize it had been used for quite that long...


Unrelated, you mentioned in your response to Jay Dee that you're working on the sequel to The Celtic Golden Dawn. I'm curious how far along it is, and how big it is(how many years worth of work)? Not that I'm ready for it, I've barely started on the first, its just exciting/daunting to think about...

And I certainly understand if you're not ready to share that info yet.


Thank you again for these magic Q&A sessions. With how much you write, read, and get done in general I'm sure you could find other uses for your mondays than answering questions like this.

-Jason P.

replies.title: Re: Why pentagrams?
replies.content:
I actually have several sequels in process. The one I mentioned in the response, provisionally titled The Celtic Cabala, is a couple of years away from publication -- I always field test all the practices in a book before sending it to the publisher, and this one has about three years of meditations and other practices in it. Another year should see the practices done, and then it's a matter of writing it and taking it through the publishing process, which isn't fast.

I'm currently working on another project using the Celtic Golden Dawn material for a cycle of seasonal rituals of self-initiation, and that'll be finished in a few months. I'm also looking at a book of natural magic using the CGD system, which should be a fun project. I'll make announcements as things come out.

As for Magic Monday, well, I know how much I would have benefited from them if someone else had been doing it back when I was studying -- and it also hasn't escaped my attention that when I do one of these, a bunch of people put tips into the tip jar at my blog, so it's not entirely unremumerative!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Another question: I normally don't spend much time with pop culture, but my wife and I sometimes do watch TV series. We finished watching Stranger Things, a Spielberg-esque series set in the 80s which uses an enormous amount of pop culture references from that time, which is when I was a child. Now, I know that the makers of the show use nostalgia on purpose to make it popular, and that the life depicted in the series is idealized. But it has still 'cast a spell' over me to a great degree, that days later, I have been afflicted with a profound bittersweet sense of nostalgia, which is strange because I'm sentimental about a childhood that I never actually experienced. It's also rather annoying (and perhaps a bit humorous) that since then I have had 80s pop music stuck in my head on repeat. It's become very apparent that pop culture really does 'change consciousness'.

It led me to wonder what nostalgia actually is, and why pop culture finds it so effective? An enormous amount of pop culture today uses settings from the recent past rather than coming up with anything new or appropriate to the time we are living in. It's like pop culture seems to want to use nostalgia to hide our present situation (shifting of the balance of power of nations, climate change, etc) rather than actually deal with.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: My take is that that's exactly what's going on. Pop culture encourages you to hide in the past rather than face the present and the future, and a lot of people are more than willing to meet it halfway, because the present sucks and the future is going to suck a lot worse.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nostalgia has no risks, because it is in the past, and the past is known. Creating the future on the other hand is full of risk and uncertainty.

One of the greatest ironies of neo-liberal capitalism was that it was supposed to make us all into rugged risk takers but it's actually made us all extremely anxious and scared. After all, who wants to fail when there's no safety net?

Pop culture is like a snake eating it's own tail, and it's going to reach the head soon. We're already up to the late 80s/early 90s. We're going to run out of decades to remake movies from.

comments.title: Neurological disorders and magic
comments.content:
I'm very concerned about my daughter, 32, who has Tourette's Syndrome, severe OCD, Executive Function Disorder and other related symptoms of neurological disorders (she also had seizures as a child). She studied hypnotherapy, NLP, and other things through the internet on her own for years to use the various techniques to manage her hallucinations, tics, violent impulses, etc. She refuses to take drugs. It's been a long, hard road for her.

We were talking about her recent discovery and enthusiasm for the Wayseer Manifesto people. It looks to me like a disguised system of magic that is newly invented and isn't tied to a tradition, and all your warnings come screaming in my mind. She is describing what sounds like magic forces flowing through her, both positive and negative. What concerns me is that she cannot block the negative forces. Given her neurological reality, she says it requires too much energy to block. Instead, she acknowledges the energies, lets them flow through her without letting them alter her, like the Litany against Fear in Dune, then lets them leave her. Thinking back now, I wonder if these flowing forces has been her reality all her life, and all the constant weird things her body does physically and mentally are forces she is wide open to. She cannot stop them she says. And she is now experiencing powerful shudderings like a Kundalini awakenening.

I try really hard to understand what her reality is like, but I struggle. I take some comfort in knowing that because she dealt with a lot of hypnotists online, total strangers, she developed a core "safe place" where her mind retreats to at will, and from which, if things got out of hand, she could regain control at any time she chose. She spent years strengthening this, and could be how she allows the forces to flow through her without touching her.

I wanted to teach her the SOP to block these negative forces, but she refuses, saying the ideas and words are different and clash with her mind's system, which is unique and has evolved to deal with her issues. Not surprisingly, I'm rather worried about what might go through her unchecked. Over the years, she has gained some mastery over herself and is experiencing fewer meltdowns and "storms" as she calls them, where her entire neurological system goes completely haywire physically, mentally, and emotionally. She seems to be going in the right direction, but I worry about whether the Wayseers might lead her into something harmful to her.

Any thoughts on all this? I'm powerless to help her anyway, but maybe I shouldn't worry and just let her walk her path?

Myriam

replies.title: Re: Neurological disorders and magic
replies.content: Since you can't help her, you probably do need to let her walk her path. Let her know that you're there for her when she needs you, and leave it at that -- really, that's all you can do.

replies.title: Re: Neurological disorders and magic
replies.content: Thank you. I really do appreciate being able to bounce ideas off someone who is vastly more knowledgeable than I am.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content: I was starting on the Geomancy book and I was wondering about the references to inner element and outer element.  It wasn't obvious if there is a way you can determine this by looking at the pattern or whether it is predetermined by other means and you just need to memorise it?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: It's something I got out of the traditional lore, and I haven't been able to work out how it's determined, so yes, you probably just need to commit it to memory.

comments.title: CGD Ovate question
comments.content:
Good morning, JGM!
Thank you for all that you have chosen to share with us.

In my meditations on the on the 16 geomantic figures and their elemental relationships, I have reached a puzzlement: the aetherial plane and body seems associated with both water and air; and the intellectual with both air and fire.

Fire = spiritual essence/primal spark
Air = intellectual
Water = aetherial
Earth = material

Ceugant = intellectual plane, triangle of fire
Gynfydd = aetherial plane, triangle of air
Abred = material plane,triangle of earth and triangle of water together.

Is Water both material and aetherial; Air both aetherial and intellectual; Fire both intellectual and the spark of spiritual essence?
Or am I over-thinking this lesson?
Any wisdom you care to share is most valued...

replies.title: Re: CGD Ovate question
replies.content: The lesson to take from this is that correspondences aren't identities. It's not that water "is" aetherial, it's in certain senses, when looked at in certain ways, the aetherial plane relates to the other planes the way that water relates to the other elements. Does that help?

replies.title: Re: CGD Ovate question
replies.content:
That does help! I will continue meditating on that...
Many thanks again for sharing your wisdom.
May the ancient starlight shine upon you and your loved ones...

comments.title: Triads/Ternaries
comments.content:
I've been continuing to explore and meditate on this potential correspondence between the Tree of Life (sans Daath) and the triad of druidic elements. Unsurprisingly, triad relationships keep popping out of the woodwork, so to speak, but I want to be cautious and not force or shoehorn relationships into a pattern to which they do not actually belong. (Am I finding these triads just because I am looking for them?)

One of the correspondences I've been looking at in this process is the polarity of being/becoming, rest/motion, stasis/change. In the "usual" view, this is a binary -- each being a pole. Is there any tradition of a ternary here? In meditating on the seasonal correspondences of the elements, I noticed that calas/winter/stasis and nwyfre/summer/change were joined with gwyar/spring-autumn...transition? That is, the third component is the state of transition between stasis and change?

Obviously, more meditation is in order here, but I was just wondering if this matches any tradition that you know of.

Thanks!

David, by the lake

replies.title: Re: Triads/Ternaries
replies.content: Yes, you're finding them because you're looking for them -- that's the point. If you learn to look for triads, you'll find them more often, and you'll get stuck in binaries less often. As for the polarity of being/becoming and so on, good! That's a very useful theme for meditation. May I make a suggestion? Being/beginning/becoming...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Are there any esoteric exercises or rituals that can open up a person's intuition and ability to understand and respond to the emotions and psychological needs of others? Reading Berne and Steiner lately have made me aware of how tone deaf I am to the messages being sent by others.  As a result I often find that I allow myself to resort to needless game playing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Steiner's book How To Know Higher Worlds has a range of useful exercises. The other things I'd suggest are regular meditation and daily divination, both of which tend to develop subtle perceptions in a balanced and healthy fashion.

comments.title: Saturn's Influence
comments.content:
I would like to develop a deeper understanding of the influence of Saturn in my natal chart and wonder if there are specific techniques you would recommend to work with this energy. I understand that Saturn is a malefic which has alarmed me no end. I have Saturn (17 degrees) conjunct Sun (16 degrees) in 9th house Libra, both square ascendant Capricorn. I have been told that this is a difficult configuration made all the worse by another square of Moon in 6th house Cancer. And Neptune (another malefic?) is also conjunct Sun / Saturn in 9th house Libra at 21 degrees. Do you have any advice, suggestions, practices, readings that would help me understand and appreciate these planetary influences?
Also, do you have a limit as to how many questions any one person can ask per Magic Monday? (I have so many!)
Thank you so much for all that you do.
Yanocoches in Colorado

replies.title: Re: Saturn's Influence
replies.content:
Saturn is a malefic, sure, but it can also work to your benefit. Liz Greene's Saturn: A New Look at an Old Devil is a good book to read in this light. Neptune in my view is a benefic, not a malefic -- it shares a planet with Jupiter, after all -- and Saturn in Libra is exalted, thus on his best behavior. I'd encourage you to get a complete natal chart delineation from a local astrologer -- you've got plenty of them in Colorado! -- and have him or her walk you through your chart to see how the strengths and weaknesses work together. Trying to make sense of a chart based on just a few planets and their positions is like trying to make sense of a piece of music by noticing only the B flat notes.

As for questions, two or three is a pretty good number; many more than that and I may get grumpy.

comments.title: Hi It’s Me Pogonip
comments.content: I have one for you.  I have instructions to make up a charm to bring in needed money.  The instructions tell you to treat the charm as a living thing.  What would you feed it?  I drew it on an index card, I can’t very well water it.  I’m stumped.

replies.title: Re: Hi It’s Me Pogonip
replies.content: Do it the way you would feed any other guest -- put a portion of food on a little plate, or in a little bowl, and set it before the charm so that the charm can eat as much as it wants; then, after a while (say, the length of an ordinary mealtime) take away what it hasn't eaten and do with it what you do with any other unwanted food. That's how you make offerings in  many cultures!

replies.title: Re: Hi It’s Me Pogonip
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: Religio-imperial occultism
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A central argument of my forthcoming book (in Brill's Iran Studies series) is that the epochal shi'ization of a very Sunni Iran in the 16th and 17th centuries was carried out via explicitly occult-scientific means, among others. While your CGD system may well be utilized to imperially druidize North American society in coming centuries (gods willing!), what other instances of such successful occultist retooling of mainstream religious culture within an imperial context do you suggest I could point to as useful historical parallels? The equally epochal, if far bloodier, protestantization of parts of western Europe was not, of course, particularly occult-scientific in tenor -- to the contrary; but surely other similar Western transformations were?

replies.title: Re: Religio-imperial occultism
replies.content: Valerie Flint's The Rise of Magic in Early Medieval Europe suggests that the Christianization of Europe was very powerfully furthered by the magical dimension of Christian practice. The Protestantization of Europe had a magical dimension -- I'd encourage a reading of Frances Yates' The Rosicrucian Enlightenment as a way to get a sense of this. You might also look at the way that Dion Fortune set out to transform attitudes toward sex in the English-speaking world with a series of magical workings, some of which took the form of published novels, others of which took the form of publicly presented rituals -- and her order also took the lead in using magical means to counter Nazi Germany, as discussed in The Magical Battle of Britain.

comments.title: Ghostly presence in new home
comments.content:
After I moved to a new place last year I started to feel, neigh even see or hallucinate, the presence of an entity or entities in our new home. That surprised me because I’m not one to experience such things and not even sure what I believe in that regard. At first, I just felt the presence but now I see or hallucinate them, usually as vague distortions in the air or wisps but occasionally as a nearly distinct object or body. During the first few months there were just a few instances then it became a daily experience that went on for weeks. My house is a mere 640 sq. ft. so avoidance is not a possibility.

I can attribute no emotion or other quality except presence to the experience. It is sort of like having someone you don’t know in a cubicle next to you at work. You know they are there but other than that nothing specific comes of it. Several times a day I engage in ritual prayer and it if often noticeable as an apparently respectful but distracting presence just behind me and to the left.

My question, not having familiarity with such things, is what if anything should I do? Should I be concerned and try to banish it or ask it to leave? Or, given its apparently peaceful disposition, should I coexist with it? Do such things normally go away on their own accord? My house is a brand new, very small, off-grid place on the edge of a wet meadow at the end of a mile of private dirt road in the Maine woods. Not an ancient castle or an old New England farmhouse where the romantic in me expects to hear these stories. Just a very small modern house surrounded by secondary forest, wetlands and a nearby railroad track.

I should state that as of two weeks ago whatever this was or was not simply ceased but I’m still curious what the John Michael Greer or his readers think one should do when confronted with such a presence. It hardly seems to call for fear or revulsion but on the other hand I generally prefer to be alone when I’m alone.

replies.title: Re: Ghostly presence in new home
replies.content: I'd encourage you to coexist with it, so long as it shows no signs of interfering with you or others. You are never actually alone, by the way.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I've heard a bit about the Saturn return with regards to a natal chart. Is there a way to calculate when that would happen using the free natal chart services I've seen around the internet? What should one be on the lookout for when they are in their Saturn return? Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is easy. Your Saturn return usually happens near your 30th birthday, and then again near your 60th and (if you should have one) your 90th. To figure it out, go to a natal chart service and get your natal chart. Then pick up an ephemeris or an astrological calendar and see where Saturn is now. If, say, your Saturn is in the early degrees of Capricorn, you'll be right on a return now, since Saturn moves from the 4th to the 7th degree of Capricorn this month; if not, you can flip forward or back to figure out when Saturn will be in the same position as it was when you were born -- that's what a Saturn return is, after all.

comments.title: Questions
comments.content:
1 – What protective herbs would you use to surround parsley ?
2 – Several tradition advise for a period of fasting or at least an abstinence of some foods for a certain period. In your perspective is that merely a matter of discipline or is it a form of purification? – 2.1 – It it is a purification it is important for a magician to do that regularly ?
3 – You advised several times the use of banishment rituals, but I don’t recall that you’ve ever advised shielding (apologies if I am wrong). You don’t consider shielding to be useful?
4 – What is you take on Reiki, if any?

Thank You

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content:
1) I'd look up protective herbs in a suitable book, such as my Encyclopedia of Natural Magic, and decide which ones I want in my garden.

2) Depends on the fine details of the practice. As to whether it's important, that depends on what kind of magical work you're doing and whether you find fasting to be helpful.

3) Depends on what you mean by shielding. That's not a concept you'll find in traditional occultism.

4) I don't have one. I haven't looked into it.

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content: Thank You Sir

replies.title: Re: The Templars

Now, on another aspect of my life, It's a very common topic with my therapist that we talk about my relationship with girls. I had a girlfriend six years ago and it was the second and last time I ever had sex. I tend to feel guilt when I express my sexuality. I don't really feel confortable when I want to express a girl that I like her, so I don't tend to come off as attractive.
I investigated my natal chart and turns out that I have a mars-venus squaring, with venus conjuncted with the sun in Aries in the third house, while mars is in Cancer in the sixth. My guessing is that the sun settles the match for venus, making me emotional, while mars (trined with mercury in pisces) makes me be agressively rational, but cannot manifest much in action. So I looked up martian herbs and I'm thinking a basil and thyme infusion can help my mars get in tune. Is my assessment accurate? What else could I try or consider?
Thanks in advace.
Juan Pablo (with the little snail avatar on the blog)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd encourage you to get a full birth chart delineation from a competent astrologer, because that kind of "well I have this, and this means that" tends to be very misleading. If your sex life is a mess, that's not something you can deal with by drinking herbs; you need to figure out why your feelings on the subject are tangled up, and resolve those tangles, or you'll just be putting more energy into an already tangled situation.

comments.title: starting a practice
comments.content:
Many thanks for hosting Magic Mondays! They are very helpful.

is it normal to have a range of issues come up when one starts doing banishing rituals? Most notably after starting to do the Sphere of Protection I've been fatigued and depressed with pain in my heart and solar plexus. A back injury immediately behind my heart which I correlate to a a lifetime of unresolved chronic grief alternately relaxes and I am restored to full range of motion and then contracts and is much tighter with a lot of cracking.

Is this sort of thing to be expected? I really enjoy doing the rituals and feel great happiness as I do the visualizations. Afterwards, though, there is melancholy and fatigue. When I first started I slept 15 hours a day for three days in a row. It seems like these rituals may be balancing some of my extremes, but I feel it is prudent to check in to see if this sort of thing is a flashing danger sign.

replies.title: Re: starting a practice
replies.content: Quite the contrary, it's a sign that your system is trying to recover. I suspect you have a vast amount of physical tension and don't get enough rest. Try responding to the melancholy and fatigue by taking a nap, if that's an option for you, and see how that works.

What do you think about the DMH system's ritual and energetic practice looking back? Is the CGD system simply another option, or does it represent significant ways that your view has changed? If so, how?

Thank you very much!
Yucca glauca

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The Druid Magic Handbook system is entirely viable; it's just that I prefer a Golden Dawn-style practice, so that's the option I use these days.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: How might I begin exploring Druidry while also firmly on a different spiritual path that I most certainly do not want to abandon?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on the other spiritual path. Some blend fairly well with Druidry, some don't. (There are, for example, plenty of Christian Druids.) Without further data I can't really suggest anything.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hellenic polytheism :)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You should be fine. Back in the day, Greeks and Romans who traveled to Celtic countries made offerings to Celtic deities as a matter of basic courtesy, and Celts who came to visit the Greek colonial city of Massilia (today's Marseille) did the same thing in return. Polytheism is helpful that way. ;-) Just maintain all your current devotions and other spiritual practices toward the gods and goddesses of Hellas, and leave half an hour or so at least between those practices and your Druid practices (to let your energy body settle back into a normal condition), and there should be no trouble at all.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you :)

comments.title: Establishing a relationship with the divine
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I've been praying in the hopes of establishing a relationship with a god or goddess. I believe it was a comment on the Ecosophia blog where you once recommended the prayer: "I believe and hope that there is a god or goddess who is interested in working with me, and I would like to make contact with that deity." I've used this prayer ever since I came across it weeks ago. I feel as though something is listening to me each time I pray, but either I haven't recognized or been given a sign that my prayer is being answered. I'm just not sure what to expect from this, and perhaps my yearning is getting in the way. What do you recommend?

Many thanks,
Tim

replies.title: Re: Establishing a relationship with the divine
replies.content: No, you just haven't taken the next step. When you get the sense that someone is listening, greet that someone and ask if he/she will identify himself/herself to you. Go on to have a conversation if possible, remembering that the other party's words will likely take the form of thoughts suddenly surfacing in your mind, like stray thoughts -- words of thunder et al. are not too common these days.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG
I know you do not do dream interpretations. My own dreams seem mostly obvious - if sometimes difficult - or they are incomprehensible. However, recently they have got more interesting and fewer are unwelcome repetitions.

I have been confronted a few times over a decade or so by a bull - gray or 'whitish' - a somewhat challenging presence when he appears. This time there were intriguing differences. I was charged with delivering with thanks a delayed payment to another farmer - the delay explicitly not a problem - for a bull previously purchased from the owner of the valuable progenitor bull. I was challenged on arrival at the farm, but this time by a younger bull set to guard the place. He instantly answered however to the farmer's quiet voice, off stage as it were. There was a rather rag-tag crowd shouting in the road behind me, so I welcomed the protective animal and its instant recognition and goodwill.

I am not a devotee of the rather convoluted classical Greek myth but wondered whether my literary imagination was working overtime.I had just delved into my reactions to Europe on your Ecosophia blog. Older imaginations have spent some effort on this kind of stuff, so my complaint of lacking a 'mythos' or two, if that is a word, might have roused a response. Any thoughts?

best
Phil H

replies.title: (no subject)

Am fascinated by the antiquity of the star images going perhaps back to the palaeolithic. I like the connection with vernal equinox. Heavens, I'm ignorant!

best
Phil H

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I find it fairly clear to me that a great many people are emotionally scared by the decisions made in the 1970s and especially the 1980s to abandon the possibility of transforming our civilization into a sustainable one. This makes sense to me from a non-magical point of view, except for the following:

1: This scaring affects people born after the decisions were made. I can think of a few things that I would say look like emotional scaring related to these decisions, but which affect not just baby boomers, but a lot of millennials as well.

2: This is true of people who I can't imagine have any idea what they did, whether because I doubt their intelligence, or their religious views block these thoughts from even occurring.

Is it possible this is the result of an emotional scar in the national egregor? Does a national egregor even exist?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: When parents treat something as a taboo topic, children raised in their household absorb that taboo nonverbally from infancy on. The extent of the national nervous breakdown here in the US in the early 1980s was severe enough that I'd expect things to echo for generations, except among those who bring the whole thing to full consciousness and deal with it. Did you ever read Watership Down? Do you recall the scenes at the warren of the shining wire? It's that sort of thing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Might part of it relate to reincarnation? I'd imagine the reincarnation of (ex) Ronald Reagan could easily have a very guilty conscience right now

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Also a possibility.

replies.title: Re: Blood donation

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A few weeks back I came up with the acronym WODN for something I was doing at work. I thought I might as well make Woden out of it and left it at that. However, over the next few days I kept coming across the name Woden/Odin. I then remembered something you had said about how the gods try to communicate with you, so I decided to go into my sacred grove to see if I could talk to him. Sure enough in the grove a dark figure with a grey beard appeared in the south. He had real presence like one of those people who can change the mood in a crowded room even without saying a thing. We drank imaginary mead, which I offered him, and I could clearly taste it and smell the honey. We talked about communication in general and he asked me about my values and my motivation. Words just seemed to form in my mind rather than me thinking what to say. Still, I had the idea that he was testing me. Since then, I have tried to talk again, but without much success. Perhaps I failed his test.

Thinking back on this, I'm not sure if I wasn't just fooling myself. Woden/Odin is not in the Celtic pantheon I follow. I suppose the nearest equivalent would be Lugh. I have had the feeling of being guided by Cerridwen, but never such a one-on-one conversation.

What do you think, was I fooling myself? Did I miss something obvious? Any advice would be much appreciated!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Woden is like that. Everyone I know who's worked with him has said that he shows up when he wants to, not when you want him to. The Celtic equivalent of Woden, by the way, is Gwydion ap Don -- the names are cognate.

comments.title: 2 more questions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Last week I asked about suicide, and after mulling over your answer, I have a follow up question: Does Druidry have any specific thoughts on the matter of reaching the end of one's life gracefully?
I can't help but feel there's got to be something more sane than modern society's hard slog through hospitals and nursing homes.

Also, in a reply to another commenter, you mentioned Ahrimanic and Luciferian capitals. Where did these terms come from, so I can study up?

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: Re: 2 more questions
replies.content:
Druidry doesn't have a lot of specific thoughts. It's more a set of general principles that each person applies in his or her own way. One of those principles is that nature is generally smarter than you are, and the normal and natural process of ripening toward death that we call "aging" is therefore a lot less burdensome for everyone when it's treated as normal and natural.

The terms "Ahrimanic" and "Luciferian" are from the writings of Rudolf Steiner.

And generally, I also find that while the breathing in light fills me with almost bursting energy, the brethiing out feels like it's cleaning me up - am I right in assuming that's part of the purpose?

Thanks for all your help here

replies.title: Re: Light body
replies.content: That kind of thing is quite common. Don't worry about it. Yes, the light breathing is meant to energize on the inbreath and expel toxins and imbalances on the outbreath.

comments.title: Of Druid Wands and Peithynen Frames
comments.content: You mentioned toward the end of The Coelbren Alphabet that you did not include the Coelbren in The Celtic Golden Dawn, as you didn't discover the divinitory meanings of the letters until after the CGD was published. I am planning to begin solitary work with the CGD, and I was wondering if it would be okay to replace the geomantic material with the Coelbren system, or should I instead practice the system as presented in the book and add the Coelbren later when I'm more experienced? I've never practiced any magical system whatsoever, so I know this isn't the time to be freewheeling, but I'm extremely drawn to the life, story, and work of Mr. Williams (the name of his hymnal inspired the name of my journal) so I'm anxious to start work with the Coelbren whenever it's prudent to do so.

replies.title: Re: Of Druid Wands and Peithynen Frames
replies.content: If you want to do the material in the CGD, do it exactly as given in the book, including the geomancy; you can also study and practice the Coelbren at the same time. Yes, that means that you'll be practicing two divination systems at the same time. Given that initiates of the Hermetic GD had to master three systems -- geomancy, tarot, and astrology -- you're getting off easy!

comments.title: When to change magical tactics?
comments.content:
I’m engaging in an established magical practice, well supported by the traditional literature, in hopes of improving the material conditions of my life. I’ve been doing it fairly steadily for a few months, but with no discernible results so far. Should I continue, while increasing my efforts on the material plane, appeal to another deity, or try some other approach altogether?

I suppose it’s likely that I’m missing something, and may need to enquire by some form of divination, or by paying attention and developing intuition in some way I haven’t been - or both. What would be the best way to assess my situation and decide what to do?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: When to change magical tactics?
replies.content: Divination is one very useful tool, and is much more likely to be able to answer these questions than I am!

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Is there a difference in potency or ease of use between the natural crystal forms of gems and minerals and those that have been cut, cabbed, or tumbled?

thanks!
Bonnie

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know. Why not get some examples and see?

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In a prior post you referred to the Tarot system of divination as functioning a mesocosm. I've tried to find references to the use of this word in the context of divination without much luck.
Could you define mesocosm and discuss how it relates to divinatory arts?
Thank you

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The mesocosm is anything you create or apply between the macrocosm (the universe) and the microcosm (you). A tarot deck, for example, represents the universe, and it represents you; it's a mirror that reflects both of them. You won't find anything on it, because I invented the concept in 2010 and haven't yet written extensively on it.

comments.title: Irish Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram
comments.content:
Hi, JMG,

My background is mainly in Irish and Norse magical traditions and polytheism and in Appalachian folk magic, and while I've had a fondness for ritual magic for quite a while, I've not had much in the way of experience practicing it. Lately, however, I've been getting some very clear signals that I need to get back to my roots in practice connected to the Druid revival and to more structured magical practice (I am an OBOD member, though I haven't been active in the Order for a few years, and while I had been developing a rich devotional practice, events over the last couple years of my life have sort of torn everything down to ground level).

To that end, I've been curious about your Celtic Golden Dawn curriculum, and if it turns out to be a good match, possibly applying for membership within the Druidic Order of the Golden Dawn. I had a question—well, make that three—regarding your post a few weeks back about polytheistic versions of the LBRP. In the post, you say to "trace a pentagram with the first two fingers of your right hand, beginning with the top point, and tracing counterclockwise to banish. (This is for the banishing ritual; you'd trace clockwise to summon.)" However, in your book Learning Ritual Magic, those (counter-clockwise from the top point) are the instructions you give for the Invoking Pentagram, whereas the Banishing Pentagram begins at bottom left and moves clockwise. I've usually heard clockwise to invoke, counter-clockwise to banish, and I assume it makes a difference, so I wonder if you could clarify? Is it a matter of translating from a Judeo-Christian frame to a polytheistic one, an error, or is there something I'm missing?

My other two questions are sort of a package deal: 1) Is the CGD curriculum capable of being translated into an Irish context? I have close relationships with the Tuatha De Danann, but most of the other Celtic deities favoured by the traditions of the Druid Revival have never really managed to become more than fond acquaintances. 2) I've been contemplating which god(desse)s would fit well the concepts invoked in the traditional LBRP, and I would be curious what you thought of my conclusions:

Invoked as patron in the Circle of Presence: AN MORRÍGAN

Pentagrams—
East (Goddess 1): DANU
South (God 1): CROM
West (Goddess 2): BRÍD
North (God 2): LUGH

In place of the elemental Archangels—
“Before me Usicias bi a Findias; behind me Semias bi a Muirias; to my right hand Esrus bi a Goiras; to my left hand, Morfesa bi a Failias. For about me stand the pentagrams, and upon me shines the blessing of (AN MORRÍGAN).” Visualize the four bardic sages of the Tuatha De Danann, bearing the four treasures (the Sword of Nuada, the Cauldron of the Dagda, the Spear of Lugh, and the Lia Fail) in the four directions when you say these words.

replies.title: Re: Irish Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram
replies.content:
The difference in the pentagrams is deliberate. The Hermetic GD does it one way, the Druidical GD does it a different way; Learning Ritual Magic is an introduction to the magic of the Hermetic GD, and should not be used as a guide to Druidical magic.

If you want to become a member of the Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn you need to do the rituals and practices exactly as given in The Celtic Golden Dawn, and yes, that includes working with the Welsh Druid divine names and the rest of it. Any work done using some other set of symbolism isn't relevant to the DOGD and cannot be used to qualify for the grades of initiation of the DOGD. That said, if you want to do something else on your own, outside the DOGD, by all means; one of the reasons the book was written the way it was, is precisely to make it easy for people to work out their own Golden Dawn-style magic using the symbolism they prefer.

comments.title: Luck
comments.content: Is it possible to have bad luck? Pop Psychology might say it's all in one's outlook, but I think some things are objectively bad, and New Age stuff might say a person is attracting bad luck with their interior mental state, but I don't think life is really all that under our control...  Mainstream science might say that there's no such thing as bad luck, but if so, I'd still like an explanation for my perceptions that tell me some people seem stuck with bad luck like it were left-handedness.

replies.title: Re: Luck
replies.content: In my experience, bad luck comes from the belief that your life isn't under your own control. People who believe that they can change their life and do things with it have good luck; people who believe that they're stuck with the lives they've got have bad luck. You can draw your own conclusions...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

The four classical Elements have simple geometric symbols, perfect for use in ritual. Are you aware of any comparable symbols for the triad of Druidic Elements? I could always make something up, but I'd rather tap into existing symbolism if possible.

If the answer is no (actually, whatever the answer) I'd be very interested in whatever thoughts you might have regarding the design of such symbols.

Respectfully,
Ynnothir Coll

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, they do. The symbol of Calas is the square; the symbol of Gwyar is the equilateral triangle, point up; the symbol of Nwyfre is the circle. They are respectively forest green, sky blue, and white.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I recently picked up Learning Ritual Magic, but I've been having issues with setting up and solidifying a consistent daily routine. Something in particular I've been struggling with is recalling the events of the day before bed, since to me most of it blurs together in a haze. Would it be advisable to keep a small daily journal to record the events of the day as they happen, to help strengthen this part of my mind?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, that would defeat the purpose of the exercise, which is to remember things using just your memory. Yes, it's difficult, but it gets easier with practice, and of course that's the point: you're developing unfamiliar skills, not trying to achieve an unrealistic level of perfection all at once.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

My name is Grey. I've been reading your material, including many of your books, for years but generally do not comment. That may be changing in the future. Thank you for all the work you do.

My question this week is in regards to your book The Art and Practice of Geomancy. I can't help but notice that the magical system outlined there is rather different from AODA, HGD, and DOGD. I think I understand why you did it that way, as you were deliberately drawing exclusively (or almost so) on Renaissance sources, but the end result seems to be that the ritual practice outlined therein isn't as... energetically robust, as your other systems.

I'm considering beginning work primarily with the material from your Geomancy book, and would like to develop the three pillars of practice you are always talking about: meditation, divination, and ritual.

I'm wondering though, and I've been trying to work it out myself but can't quite fit the pieces together: Is there a more "modern" style banishing ritual that you believe would work well with the correspondences and entities in the Geomancy book? Would a SoP work? Would the LBRP? If so, do you have any pointers regarding directional correspondences or divine names? Or would you advise that this is too much mixing of systems for a beginner?

Thank you in advance for any input you may have.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: For beginners, any mixing of systems is a mistake. Start by choosing one system, and study and practice it until you're good at it. That way you get trained in a complete and coherent magical system, rather than the loose ends and scraps that happen to appeal to you! Once you've learned one magical system from top to bottom, you can go and start the mix-and-match game with good results.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection + Meditation
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer

I try to combine the Sphere of Protection with Meditation in the following way.
1. Perform the Elemental Cross.
2. Express gratitude to gods and elements and pray to them.
3. trace out the four elements for banishing while tracing out the limit of the sphere with my finger.
4. Spin the bands of light into a Sphere.
5. Sit in meditation within that Sphere.


I have been trying this for the past 3 or so days, with minor results that could be attributed to other factors. Am I missing something?

I am also at a loss as to how to combine invoking and banishing in the same ritual. If, for example, I want to invoke Fire to provide me courage for that day how should I go about it ?

1. Open ritual
2.banish elements
3. Invoke Fire.
4. Form Sphere of Protection

Or
1. Open ritual
2. invoke Fire, or other Elements,
3. Form Sphere of Protection

Clearly there are many ways to go here, but if the precise purpose of each step were clear to me, I think I'd be able to handle it better.


Lordyburd

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection + Meditation
replies.content:
First, no, you're not going to get good results after doing a magical ritual only three times. Try three hundred times, for starters! If you started playing a musical instrument, presumably you wouldn't be downcast if you couldn't get the results you wanted after just three sessions of practice; magic is exactly the same way.

Now, as for the Sphere of Protection, please reread the instructions. You never just invoke one element. You invoke all seven, asking each of them for whatever you need, and banish all four material elements, releasing everything you wish to get rid of, every single time you do the ritual. So it's like this...

1. Perform the Elemental Cross to open
2. Invoke and banish with air
3. Invoke and banish with fire
4. Invoke and banish with water
5. Invoke and banish with earth
6. Invoke spirit above, spirit below, and spirit within
7. Form the sphere via the circulation of light

When you invoke with air, you trace the invoking symbol and ask the powers of air for what you need from air, and then trace the banishing symbol and banish from yourself whatever you want to release. You do the same thing with each of the other material elements, and then you invoke with the three forms of spirit. Every. Single. Time.

And you do it every day for a year, and then start paying attention to whether you're getting results. I hope that makes things a little clearer...

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection + Meditation
replies.content: Forgive me for the oversight, I forgot to mention that I do use all the elements and spirits. I have been practicing for a year, but this is the first time i’m Intentionally using this to clear space for meditation.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: How do the magical elements map to the material elements? Is there such a thing as a Molybdenum elemental?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: They don't. The same word is being used in two different senses; back in the 18th century, chemists took the word "element" (which until then had referred to fire, water, earth, and air) and assigned it to the chemical elements then being discovered, causing no end of confusion.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Does it ever. Okay, so how does the difference between them unpack?

comments.title: theurgy
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

As I researched Neo-platonic philosophy, I came across theurgy.
Is "Learning Ritual Magic...." a good primer for that subject,
please?

Thanks,

mac

replies.title: Re: theurgy
replies.content: Depends on what you mean. Classical theurgy? I don't know of a good primer on that. Modern theurgy? Any book on ceremonial magic will do you. Decide which tradition you want depending on which gods you're comfortable invoking; if you're fine with the Judeo-Christian god, then yes, Learning Ritual Magic is one workable choice.
